game_name: Musya: The Classic Japanese Tale of Horror

danger_signals.visual_form: Boss encounters with Japanese horror aesthetic
danger_signals.meaning: Supernatural threats requiring spiritual combat; each defeated boss represents a vengeful ghost requiring submission

danger_signals.visual_form: 16-unit health bar labeled with life energy concept
danger_signals.meaning: Player's life force draining; imminent game-over when depleted
Critical danger state; three lives available before permanent failure

danger_signals.visual_form: Tengumura Cavern (鬼宝鍾乳洞) - literally 'demon treasure stalactite cave'
danger_signals.meaning: Hostile supernatural territory requiring traversal through increasing danger

safety_signals.visual_form: Words 'monster defeated' (怨霊調伏 Onryō Chōfuku) + scroll reward after boss victory
safety_signals.meaning: Progress checkpoint; successful supernatural combat grants new defensive/offensive abilities

safety_signals.visual_form: Tengumura Village as initial safe haven
safety_signals.meaning: Sanctuary before descent; Mayor Akagi provides quest context and salvation purpose

safety_signals.visual_form: Imoto's collapse and greeting in village
safety_signals.meaning: Respite point before main quest; establishes homebase before venturing into cavern

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Portrayed as survivor of initial battle; sole defender descending into abyss

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Japanese yokai/ghost entities requiring spiritual submission
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple boss variations representing different categories of vengeful ghosts
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Inhabit Tengumura Cavern; represent supernatural antagonistic forces
Village location serves as quest origin; civilian authority structure intact

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Imoto described as sole survivor; all other combatants perish
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Imoto collapses and seeks refuge; triggers journey initiation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Shizuka identified as rescue target requiring descent into supernatural cavern
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Quest narrative drives protagonist downward; rescue mission is primary objective

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Supernatural infestation (abyss filled with vengeful spirits)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Progressive boss encounters with distinct spirit entities
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Each defeated Onryō triggers spiritual submission message; incremental world cleansing

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Scroll collection system; player can carry up to three spell scrolls
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss defeats grant scrolls (新しい呪文); player power progression through supernatural knowledge

environmental_storytelling.example: Tengumura Village (天狗村) references tengu mythology; Kihōshōnyūdō (鬼宝鍾乳洞) literally 'demon treasure stalactite cave' conveys supernatural wealth and danger
Imoto's survival of initial battle establishes him as exceptional; other combatants' deaths indicate world's lethality
Onryō (怨霊 vengeful ghost) terminology instead of generic 'monsters' positions game within Japanese folklore tradition
Pikeman/spearman protagonist, village mayor structure, shrine/cave architecture establish period-specific world identity

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Collapses; seeks mayor's guidance; receptive to quest briefing
Descends abyss; engages bosses; collects spell scrolls after victories
Greets Imoto; provides Shizuka rescue context; remains in village (static authority)
Boss encounters triggered at progression gates; defeated via combat; submit upon defeat (怨霊調伏)
Trapped in abyss; motivates protagonist descent; objective of quest

notes: Musya is heavily rooted in Japanese cultural signification. The game communicates danger primarily through yokai/onryō (vengeful spirit) encounters rather than mechanical enemy types. Safety is signaled through village presence and spell acquisition (representing spiritual power growth). The world-state manifests as a supernatural infestation narrative: abyss filled with vengeful spirits requiring individual submission. Japanese-specific terminology (Qi/Ki for health, Onryō for spirit type, Inochi for lives) embeds cultural horror semantics. Regional edits (manji removal, tengu genitalia censorship, kanji→numerals conversion) indicate how Western localization stripped cultural specificity. Feudal Japan setting provides environmental storytelling through location names and protagonist archetype (pikeman/spearman). Game uses incremental boss defeats to signal world-state progression toward maiden rescue.
